items.q: Is traditional music always old music?
items.correct: No - traditions are living: tunes vary as they pass between performers, and new pieces are still created within traditional styles.
items.misconception: Traditional music was all written centuries ago and never changes.
items.random_wrong: Traditional music can only be performed in museums.
items.why: Because folk music is transmitted orally, every performance can reshape it; the tradition is a process, not a fixed collection of ancient pieces.

items.q: Does call and response only happen in singing?
items.correct: No - instruments use it too: a drum master's pattern answered by the ensemble, or brass riffs answered by saxophones.
items.misconception: Call and response is only a vocal technique between a singer and a choir.
items.random_wrong: Call and response means tuning instruments before playing.
items.why: The principle is musical dialogue, whatever the sound source; recognising instrumental call and response is essential in African and Caribbean listening examples.

items.q: Does syncopation mean the performers are out of time?
items.correct: No - syncopation deliberately accents off-beats while the underlying pulse stays steady.
items.misconception: Syncopated music sounds wrong because the players have lost the beat.
items.random_wrong: Syncopation means everyone plays the same rhythm in unison.
items.why: Syncopation only works against a secure pulse; the tension between steady beat and displaced accents is the intended effect, not an error.

items.q: Steel pans are most closely associated with which style and place?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Calypso, from Trinidad. “Samba, from Portugal” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The bodhran is what kind of instrument?
items.why: It's An Irish frame drum. “A Brazilian guitar” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A song that repeats the same music for every verse uses which form?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Strophic form. “Sonata form” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Accenting off-beats against a steady pulse
items.why: Correct answer: Accenting off-beats against a steady pulse. “Gradually getting faster” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Most samba grooves are written in 2/4. How many crotchet beats are in each bar?
items.why: The real figure is 2 beats. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
